speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December 7, 1995. Gerda--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left off on the last tape mentioning that out of your friends, you are now the only one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was at this point when I, well, I was almost ready to give up. So I had not given up before. I was told they need to amputate my leg, my feet, because of frozen feet which, of course, then was not necessary. But we heard many years later, as a matter of fact, just a few years ago, that apparently there was a German doctor, one of the American doctors from the Fifth Division who wrote to Kurt, mentioned that while he was on a lunch break one day, one of the German doctors took it upon himself to amputate a girl's feet when it was not necessary. I guess it was the same one. Because there were only two doctors there. That particular doctors name was Dr. [? Simone. ?] He came and told me that my, my feet will have to be amputated. And I told him I will not have it done. So he said, well then, you know. I said, fine, whatever. I've seen what I wanted to see. And I don't, I don't want it done. Then he made a statement, which only in retrospect now I see how absolutely ridiculous it was. He said, well, then we will do something, which probably is not going to work. We will bathe your feet, alternately, in boiling water and in ice water. And if there is some sensation, it will come back, and it's going to be excruciating. But it seems to me that before suggesting amputation, they should have tried it. So obviously he was that doctor that did that. And they, they proceeded with that. At first there was no sensation. Then the sensation was excruciating, but I rejoiced, because I apparently was live in my feet. And even though I have some trouble with my feet, I have been doing a lot of and running and walking and nothing happened. I think one of the most important things, in retrospect, to stress is that it has never been sufficiently illuminated [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that nobody was there. I mean, we have seen terrible tragedies. You know, the tragedies of Vietnam, the tragedies of, of our people that were held in, you know, hostage. And I certainly know what they felt. There is nothing worse than not to know if you're going to live another day, what is going to happen. Even though other people know that their country and their families were safe, it is a horrible fate to live every day with the knowledge that you might not live to see another day. The war was over. Aside from having been in the hospital where American army doctors took care of us-- but we knew that they are going to move on-- there was really no one to give any extended physical care. There were no psychiatrists, no psychologists, nobody debrief us from the trauma of six years, all young girls. There were no support systems. Do you know, in any type of tragedy, there is a support system. Nobody was-- no country wanted us. But worst of all, there was no family waiting. And we were all young girls, practically children. I was 15 when the war broke out. My friends were about the same age. Our families were killed, our homes were no longer. Nobody had any family, no place to go back to. Everybody started anew. In a new country, with new language, with skills. Most of my friends registered to go to Palestine. Illegally, of course, because there was no state of Israel, yet. They went and started to fight. They went to go to Cyprus behind barbed wire, to enter Israel, which was Palestine then, illegally on boats, to fight again. And somehow they managed to put a life together, to build families. I think it is rather incredible [INAUDIBLE]. I was more fortunate than most people, because I met Kurt on Liberation Day. And he is responsible, not only for my liberation, but for, for putting me together, for his understanding and for his love and for his caring and for making me feel that I must go on, that there's something to go on for. Because, at that point, I was, I was truly ready to give up. Somehow, though nobody told me, I had a feeling that the crutch which was that my parents would survive, I suddenly realized that in all likelihood they did not survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, but looking back-- and I think it's an important factor also to say-- that imagination helped a great deal. I remember one time on the death march, I took-- there was a very bitter day, cold and icy and no food. I spent entire day trying to visualize the party which I would give after the war. And I had the dilemma if I want a red velvet dress or a blue velvet dress. And it became of utmost important to me, and I couldn't resolve it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how you stop away hunger. I think if you had imagination, you might have had an easier time. If you faced the reality, the bitter reality of what's there, I don't think you had much of a choice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in the hospital for several months, Kurt visited me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then he made arrangements for me and my friends to, to leave, when the Russians occupied there. I went to Munich. I was fortunate that they needed people who spoke-- could read and write German, and who were anti-Nazi. And I qualified on that account. I got a job at the Civilian Censorship Division. Lots of mail had been captured in the post offices. And we had to read that mail. And if we found something, the people said, you know, they wrote home, destroy my Nazi identification, et cetera, et cetera. We followed this up and gave it to the CIC. I, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On September of-- and my relationship, of course, with Kurt intensified. I, I was very much in love with him, but I didn't know if perhaps he was married or engaged. We never talked about that. And I would never want to-- to hang the burden of that on him. I felt that he befriended me. And we will leave it at that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He sort of became the center of my life there. He brought me the first letter from my uncle in Turkey. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had prayed that there had been news, there, that perhaps he's heard from my brother. When I opened the letter, my uncle asked me where they were. So it was pretty bitter. I did not want to go to displaced persons camp. I didn't want to do that. I wanted, I wanted to start working. And then I worked for the Civilian Censorship Division. But I pretty soon saw that it was a mockery of things there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got another job. I got a job for the [INAUDIBLE] which was sort of a-- they were people, you know, from all over Europe that gathered and came for help. And it was helpful that I spoke both, you know, Polish and some Yiddish and German. And I worked there, but actually that came later.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got out of the hospital, I think it must have been at the end of July, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I was in Munich already when Germany capitulated. It was August, the war was over. And around the 12th, 13th of September, Kurt came as often as he could. I saw him. And he came this particular day. And he said he needed to talk to me urgently. And we took a walk. And he said to me, I got orders. I'm going home. Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just about managed to say that I wish him well. And I'll never forget what he did for me, and I will be forever grateful for that. I just about managed that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he said, is that all you have to say to me? And I'm wondering, why is he torturing me, you know. So I said, well, what, what do you want me to say? And he said, well, I would like you to come to America. I said, well, what would I do there? And he said, for starters, you could marry me. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the happiest moment of my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then he told me that he'll leave the decision to me. He said he could go home and try to see where he wants to live. If it's you know, he didn't have a job. If it would be New York or Buffalo, he had lived in Buffalo before. Or he said, he could enlist in the Army for a couple of more years. Because I fell, ironically, under the same laws as German civilians. And there was no fraternization with Americans. But he felt that he would get dispensation if he stated that we would be able to get married. But it would mean we would stay in Germany, probably for a year or two. And he said the decision has to be mine. It's probably the most unselfish decision I ever made in my life. Just when I realized that I belonged to someone, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew he would leave. And I didn't know when I would see him again. But I felt that I know he wanted to go home. So I told him, why don't you go home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was pretty tough, when he left two days later. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But you know, people always ask me, did-- didn't you think that he might not come back, or something? And I, it never entered my mind. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I knew he didn't have to ask me to marry him, but he wanted to ask me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he has [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the trust and all the love that I had for him and have for him. He's never disappointed. And I think I am very blessed to be able to say that after 50 years. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went back. It became a very difficult period. I was left behind in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to make a long story short, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he got in touch with my uncle in Turkey. And they put all their powers together to try to get me out of Germany. There were no consulates in Germany. And finally, I, it's a long story. I went to France through a whole chain of circumstances. And then both my uncle and Kurt felt that enough is enough already. So Kurt got back to France. How long, I think it would take another night to tell that story. To make a long story short, Kurt came back. We were married in Paris. And I came back to the states with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married on the 18th of June of 1946. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came to the States. We flew, which was incredible in those days. And, we went, first, then we went to England. We had a honeymoon on the Riviera, in Cannes. And from then, then we went back to Paris. And then we went to London. Kurt had family in England. We visited Kurt's family. And I came to the United States on the 30th of August, 1946 [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and to Buffalo, New York, on the 13th of September. Precisely a year to the day, on which we got engaged. We flew from London to-- you know, in those days, you had to stop in Newfoundland and in Ireland, Newfoundland. And I shall never forget my first view of New York. It was dawn was breaking. It seemed like-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that flight over the ocean was so turbulent, and everything was like the years in Europe. And then there was dawn and New York. And as the plane was coming down, Kurt put his arms around me, and he said, you have come home. It's been a good home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One very strange thing happened. And perhaps this is the thing that I should mention. The war started at 9:10 in the morning on September 3, 1939, when my brother's watch fell to the floor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our last evening in Paris, on the continent for me, I said to Kurt, I want to see the Palais Luxembourg. You know, the peace conferences were being held there. And I wanted to see that because, the flags of all nations were flying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, this was going to be the war that ended all wars. There is never going to be another war. That was certain. The world has learned a lot, right? So we walked along the sand. And I, we came on a bridge. And I remember we talked. And I said to Kurt, what is it going to be like in America? Is his family going to accept me? Would I ever learn English? Above all, will I have children? That was foremost in my mind, when I'm going to be able to have children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I said, now I'm going to look back at the Palais. That's going to be my last view of Europe. And I looked back, and the clock stood at 9:10, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at night. And when I wrote my book, which I was going to call my publisher said to me we could get away with it in fiction, but an autobiography, we'll never be able to mange it. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Indeed I did. My first child's name is Vivian Elissa. Her initials are for V-Day. She is my victory over Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was born when?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born October of 1948, almost two years after I came to Buffalo. I came to Buffalo. And I have shocked more people by telling them that Buffalo is the most beautiful city in the world. I like it better than Paris or London or anything. Buffalo became a true home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the healing began there. Kurt did something else, which I think was, in retrospect, I don't know how he had the foresight of doing it. Of course we were speaking German to each other, you know. And the minute we landed in New York, he said, that's it, I'll never speak German to you again. You can speak German to me if you want to, I'm going to answer you in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, what do you mean? I mean, you know, you're the only person I can talk to. And you're not going to talk to me? He said, I don't think we should start speaking German at home. If we start, we will never finish. And in years to come, you'll be grateful for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was one of the most important and wisest decisions he ever made. And I think that probably freed me from a lot of things. I have, I sort of hold an idea that most survivors could not share their experiences until now, because they spoke in the language-- you know, most survivors married survivors. And they continued speaking in German or Polish or Yiddish, or what have you. And you cannot tell the stories in the language in which it happened. I found that out when I went back to Amsterdam. I was asked to speak at Anne Frank's home. And I met a friend of Anne Frank, her name is Frieda Menco.

speaker: interviewee
text: She asked me to do a program with her. I did it in English, no problem, then she asked me to do it in German. And I said, speak German on the radio? My god, you know, only Hitler and Goebbels spoke on the radio. I couldn't possibly do that. And she says, well, didn't you ever want to tell them in their language what you think of them? I said, well this might be interesting. I started in German, and I couldn't go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly it seemed like everything tumbled down, and it became unbearable. And I realized that you can tell about your experiences in a different language. It sort of filters out a lot of the pain. But when you do it in the language in which it happens, the reality sets in, and it is unbearable. And I think I was fortunate that I was able to speak English-- with an accent of course. But very early in the game I started writing about it, which was extensive catharsis and psychoanalysis for me. And I did it not in the language in which it happened. And I think it freed me to, to speak about it. And I think that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it, it was very beneficial in the process of healing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had other children besides Vivian?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have, we have three children. Our younger daughter, Leslie and a son Jimmy and eight grandchildren. And you know, I don't even want to say that we have three children. I really feel that we have six. We have been very blessed with our two sons-in-law and our daughter-in-law, who are extremely close to us, and very understanding and very supportive. And I don't like to say that they are in-laws. I think that, that they're truly children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vivian married Jim Ullman. Leslie married Roger Simon. And Jimmy married Lynn Silversmith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you presently retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a fashion. I still do quite a bit of writing and a good amount of speaking and working for some of the causes that are very dear to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But above all, I think probably one of the greatest and most supreme moments of my life was when the grandchildren were born. By a strange coincidence-- and somebody said something marvelous-- that coincidence is God's way of remaining unanimous. And that coincidence happened the 28th and 29th of June hereto had always been the most difficult days of my life. The day when I have last saw my mother and my father. And the 28th of June 1976, we still lived in Buffalo. We got a phone call that our first grandchild was born, in Phoenix. Phoenix, a symbol of hope rising from the ashes of the past. She was born late in the day, there were no more flights. So she was born on the day I last saw my father. I was in Phoenix the following morning. And I held her in my arms on the same day that my mother held me in her arms for the last time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what was that grandchild's name? Her name is Alysa. And then we had Julie and Andrew and Melissa and Lindsay and Jessica and Jennifer and Lexi. Eight of them. Seven girls, one boy. Do you know the exact fate of your parents and your brother? Did you ever learn exactly what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: About my parents, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz. But I believe that my father did not die in Auschwitz. I rather think that my father must have died of heart failure, where he worked in [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know about my brother, though I could have known. But when somebody started telling me, I said I didn't want to hear it. I wanted to keep the illusion and keep looking for him. And I still do, even though I know it's not possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you feel you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] 95% luck and probably a good deal of good physical constitution, my ski boots. Above all, being at the right place at the right time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like at this time for you to introduce your family. So I thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That will be a great joy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for  the interview. Gerda, would you introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the greatest pleasure. The man who fulfilled all my dreams, who make it possible not only on Liberation Day but the half-century since, and we hope and pray that we will be blessed with a few more years with our family, with our children and grandchildren and freedom and blessed with the good fortune which we had to-- to have them, my husband Kurt. My granddaughter, Jessica Simon, Leslie's daughter. My granddaughter, Lindsay Ullman and Andrew Ullman, the only grandson with seven granddaughters. My oldest daughter Vivian, of whom I spoke before, VE Day, my victory over Europe. My son-in-law Jim Ullman. My daughter, Leslie Simon, who came in for that very special moment. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is not present?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately our son Jim is not present here, he's in Washington with his wife Lynn, his two children, Jennifer and Lexi. Leslie's husband Roger could not be with us today, neither my granddaughter Melissa, their middle daughter, nor their oldest daughter Julie, who is in college. Our oldest granddaughter Alysa Ullman is also not here. She's having finals in college. And I guess the rest is assembled here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you one question, before I give the opportunity for your family to speak. Gerda, what advice do you have to your children, your grandchildren, and future generations, after having lived through the Holocaust? What advice would you like to give?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess my children and grandchildren are fully cognizant of, of what I've always believed in. The family has always been the strongest support in adversity. And I think when I realized that I did not have a family, I was fortunate to meet Kurt on that day, which give us a family again. And they have been the balm on all the wounds. I think our children and grandchildren know that the love of family and the unity of family is the support system that can withhold many storms. I think it is appreciation of what we have that must always be upper mind in our minds. And above all, there is something which I have strongly believed in, is to never give up. Even when it's darkest hour, one must always hold onto hope. God knows that I had little reason not to give up. And when I look at my family and the blessings that have been ours, I marvel at the thought of what is possible if you cling to life and if you have hope, if you think of your family and above all, if you have freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to give the opportunity now for family members to say something to your mother and to your father, as children and grandchildren of survivors. Who would like to go first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I do want to say that meeting Gerda was the realization of-- of a dream that I had never dared hope would come true, of finding exactly the right person for me, and the person with whom to build a new life and who made all this possible, again. And in that sense, I feel more blessed than most people.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I guess as the oldest child, I would like to say, Mom and Dad, that I'm naturally so proud of your many, many accomplishments. I think you've touched so many lives over the years, as you shared your story, with such dignity. I think Mom and Dad have shown that one can overcome adversity. And they've been able to channel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the experiences that they had during the war years into a very positive learning experience. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they have been wonderful role models, not only to their children and grandchildren, but to, frankly, countless thousands of others, who have heard their story. And I think, because they've been able to make their own experiences relevant to the problems that people share today, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that it has, has touched something in people of all ages. You know, Dad, in recent years, you've had the opportunity to be able to tell your family's story-- there is a documentary-- and to reach out to audiences all over the country. And Mom, you certainly have a gift, which, it's been probably now, what, almost 40 years in speaking and writing about the Holocaust, that you have made this your life's work. So I think you should take great solace in knowing that you've really made a difference in this world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who goes next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead. The outlaws will go later. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as the middle child, I have often said that I was the shy one. But through the years, you begin to realize that when you have a passion about something, you can speak and you can feel and you can understand maybe what had, in so many ways, been unspoken but had just been shown in the way Mom and Dad built a life-- lives for us in, really, one of the most secure, loving, safe, happy environments that I think any child should be privileged to know. And as a young child, I always knew that my parents were remarkable. Not only were they incredibly bright, dedicated, committed to helping other people. Opening up their home and their hearts to embrace friends that really became the family that we never knew. I'm no longer a young child. 22 years later, of marriage and privileged also to have three children, do I understand now what they had to overcome and how many painful things they had to put behind them. I think when you come of the type of trauma that Mom and Dad have come out of, you either come out very bitter, or you dedicate yourself to doing something very positive. Mom and Dad have always done the most positive. And although they have reached out to countless, family always came first.
